input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue with keyboard handling in .NET 10. This is a known breaking change — `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated/removed in Android 11+ and .NET MAUI has been evolving its approach.

Let me provide a concise, helpful answer based on my knowledge. In .NET 10 (which would be based on the latest Android APIs), the recommended approach is to use the `Edge-to-Edge` display and handle insets properly, or use `KeyboardAcceleratorPlatformEffect`.

Actually, in recent .NET MAUI versions, the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was already problematic because Android itself deprecated `SOFT_INPUT_ADJUST_RESIZE` in API 30 (Android 11). The replacement approach involves using `WindowInsets` and the new `Edge2Edge` APIs.
 
For .NET 10 specifically, since MAUI moved to edge-to-edge rendering on Android, the `Resize` mode no longer works because Android deprecated the underlying `adjustResize` behavior. The better solutions are to use `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Pan` as a fallback, or leverage the newer `WindowInsetsCompat` API through platform-specific code to handle keyboard insets manually, potentially wrapping content in a `ScrollView` to accommodate the keyboard.

For a chat screen with an input bar at the bottom, MAUI's built-in `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` (available since .NET 8 and default in .NET 9/10) can handle basic scrolling, but a more robust approach involves adjusting padding based on actual keyboard height using platform-specific Android code rather than relying on deprecated manifest settings.
